fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to wrap up the native addon rebuild planner tool we've been discussing. The core idea is pretty straightforward — when someone switches the embedded runtime version in their Electron app, all those compiled native modules break and they have to manually figure out what to rebuild. It's a huge pain point we keep hearing about from devs on the forum.

The tool should be smart about which modules actually need rebuilding — like, don't rebuild dev-only stuff, and don't redo work that's already done for the right runtime. There should also be a way to force everything to rebuild if someone thinks their build cache is messed up, and a way to narrow it down to just one or two specific modules when you're iterating fast.

We also need it to handle that workspace/monorepo layout thing we dealt with on the metrics-service project — you know, where the lock file is up a couple levels from where you're actually working. And there's the scoped package naming situation that tripped us up before (the @org/package-name format).

The output format needs to be line-based and machine-parseable so CI scripts can consume it. Keep the internals clean — separate files for different concerns, similar to how we structured that login compatibility module. Don't dump everything in one file.

Can someone spec out the ABI number mapping and make sure the incremental skip logic is solid? Those are the two things I'm least confident about.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: planRebuild({ runtimeVersion, modules, dependencies, optionalDependencies, devDependencies, builtForRuntimeVersion, force, only, buildSubdir }): { runtimeAbi, selected, skipped }

trigger_keywords: what ABI does version 10.4.7 map to
how is the ABI resolved from a version string
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Runtime version 10.4.7 maps to ABI 82. The ABI resolution follows the NODE_MODULE_VERSION mapping table used by Node.js. For example, Node 3.x maps to a different ABI than Node 10.x. The tool must resolve the correct integer ABI from a semver runtime version string — this is a lookup, not a calculation.

trigger_keywords: what name does a scoped package appear as in selected list
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For scoped packages like @newrelic/native-metrics, the output uses only the short-name portion after the scope segment — so it appears as 'native-metrics' in the selected list, not the full scoped name. This applies to the comma-separated selected output and to allow-list matching.

trigger_keywords: are devDependencies included in the rebuild
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Only production dependencies (declared under 'dependencies') and optional dependencies (declared under 'optionalDependencies') are included in the rebuild closure. Modules reachable only through 'devDependencies' are excluded. This applies even if a dev dependency is a native module — it will not appear in the selected set.

The planner descends into each selected module's own declared dependencies to find transitive native children. A native child nested under a non-native parent (e.g., 'leveldown' listed under 'level' via the children array) is still selected for rebuild. The children array in the module descriptor is how privately installed sub-modules are represented.

A module is skipped when the ABI resolved from 'builtForRuntimeVersion' exactly matches the ABI resolved from 'runtimeVersion', AND the 'force' flag is not set. The 'skipped' count reflects how many selected modules were short-circuited. If the ABIs differ (e.g., a runtime upgrade), skipped is 0. If force is true, skipped is always 0 regardless of ABI match.

When the 'only' list is provided, the selected set is restricted to exactly the modules whose short-name appears in the list — regardless of their dependency category. This means a dev-only module like 'ffi-napi' CAN appear in the selected output if it is explicitly named in the 'only' list, even though it would normally be excluded.

trigger_keywords: what lock files does the resolver look for
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The root resolver recognizes exactly two lock file names: 'yarn.lock' and 'package-lock.json'. It walks upward from the starting directory, and the root is the directory that contains one of those files. If no lock file is found anywhere in the ancestry, the starting directory itself is returned as the root (output as '.').

trigger_keywords: how is the root path expressed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The project root is output as a path relative to the startDir, not as an absolute path. If the lock file is two levels up, the output is 'project_root=../..'  If the startDir itself is the root, the output is 'project_root=.' The output line format is exactly: 'project_root=<relative_path>' followed by a newline.

The read_manifest command outputs: first a line 'fields=<sorted comma-separated field names>', then one line per field in sorted order, each formatted as '<fieldname>=<JSON-encoded value>'. String values are JSON-encoded with quotes, objects are JSON-encoded inline. Example: fields=dependencies,name,version then dependencies={"ref-napi":"1.4.2"} then name="native-app" then version="1.0.0".

trigger_keywords: how does buildSubdir affect which modules are selected
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When 'buildSubdir' is provided, the selection is scoped to that sub-package's own dependency closure only. Modules declared only at the workspace root level (outside the sub-package's own dependencies/optionalDependencies) are not included. In the test case, 'sleep' is excluded because it's a devDependency, and the buildSubdir scoping means only 'snappy' (a production dep) is selected.

trigger_keywords: how should the code be organized
what files should the project have
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The project must be organized as multiple files with clear separation of concerns — do NOT collapse everything into a single file. At minimum, keep these as distinct units: graph traversal / dependency classification, ABI resolution, build-stamp comparison, project-root discovery, manifest reading, and the I/O adapter (stdin/stdout handling). The adapter translates JSON commands to core engine calls; the core engine must never read stdin or write stdout directly.

trigger_keywords: order of modules in selected list
how are selected module names sorted
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The selected module short-names in the output must be sorted alphabetically (lexicographic ascending order) and joined by commas with no spaces. For example: 'selected=bcrypt,farmhash,leveldown,native-metrics,ref-napi'. The sort applies to short-names (after stripping scope prefix for scoped packages).

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: test cases for feature1 dependency selection
show me feature1 dependency selection test cases
what are the feature1 dependency selection test inputs
give me the feature1_dependency_selection test file
what does feature1 dependency selection expect
test cases for feature2 incremental skip
show me feature2 incremental skip test cases
what are the feature2 incremental skip test inputs
give me the feature2_incremental_skip test file
what does feature2 incremental skip expect
test cases for feature3 force rebuild
show me feature3 force rebuild test cases
what are the feature3 force rebuild test inputs
give me the feature3_force_rebuild test file
what does feature3 force rebuild expect
test cases for feature4 only filter
show me feature4 only filter test cases
what are the feature4 only filter test inputs
give me the feature4_only_filter test file
what does feature4 only filter expect
test cases for feature5 project root
show me feature5 project root test cases
what are the feature5 project root test inputs
give me the feature5_project_root test file
what does feature5 project root expect
test cases for feature6 read manifest
show me feature6 read manifest test cases
what are the feature6 read manifest test inputs
give me the feature6_read_manifest test file
what does feature6 read manifest expect

context_pointers.vague_reference: that workspace/monorepo layout thing we dealt with on the metrics-service project
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The 'buildSubdir' input field in the plan_rebuild command, which scopes dependency selection to a sub-package within a monorepo. When present, only that sub-package's own dependencies/optionalDependencies closure is used for module selection, not the root workspace manifest.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the scoped package naming situation that tripped us up before
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Scoped npm packages like '@newrelic/native-metrics' must have their short-name extracted (the part after the '/' separator) for use in the selected output list and for allow-list matching. The short-name for '@newrelic/native-metrics' is 'native-metrics'.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to how we structured that login compatibility module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The multi-file, separation-of-concerns architecture described in the PRD under 'Architecture & Engineering Constraints' — specifically: separate files for graph traversal, ABI resolution, build-stamp comparison, root discovery, manifest reading, and the I/O adapter. The adapter is the only layer that touches stdin/stdout.

The NODE_MODULE_VERSION lookup table that maps Node.js runtime semver versions to integer ABI numbers. For instance, Node 10.4.7 maps to ABI 82, Node 3.x maps to a different value. This is a static lookup embedded in the ABI resolution module, not computed dynamically.

context_pointers.vague_reference: make sure the incremental skip logic is solid
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The build-stamp comparison in Feature 2: compare the ABI resolved from 'builtForRuntimeVersion' against the ABI resolved from 'runtimeVersion'. If they match and force is false, increment the skipped counter for each matching native module rather than adding it to the rebuild queue. The 'selected' list still shows all modules; 'skipped' is a count of how many of those were short-circuited.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a good question but I don't have enough technical context to answer it precisely. Could you look at the existing test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ or the PRD in start.md for the exact contract? I'd rather point you to the source of truth than give you something that conflicts with the spec.

feature1_dependency_selection.json.description: Given a project manifest plus its installed module tree, the planner selects which native modules to (re)build. It walks production and optional dependencies, descends into their transitive native children (including scoped packages nested under a parent), and excludes development-only dependencies. The output reports the resolved target ABI for the requested runtime version and the sorted set of module short-names chosen for rebuild.

feature2_incremental_skip.json.description: The planner avoids redundant work by skipping native modules already built for the exact target runtime. Each previously built module carries a build-stamp recording the architecture and ABI it was compiled for. When every module's stamp matches the requested runtime's ABI and rebuilding is not forced, all modules are skipped. If the target runtime's ABI differs from the stamped ABI (a runtime upgrade), the stamps no longer match and every module is selected for rebuild. The output reports how many modules were skipped versus selected.

feature3_force_rebuild.json.description: When the force flag is set, the planner ignores existing build-stamps and rebuilds every native module even if it was already built for the exact target runtime. No module is reported as skipped.

feature4_only_filter.json.description: An allow-list narrows the rebuild to only the named modules. Even when the project declares many native dependencies, supplying an explicit list restricts the selected set to exactly those names (matched by module short-name), ignoring all others regardless of their dependency type.

feature5_project_root.json.description: Determines a project's root directory by walking upward from a starting directory until a dependency lock file is found. The root is the directory containing the lock file. Either of two common lock-file names is recognized. When no lock file exists anywhere in the ancestry, the starting directory itself is returned as the root. Output is the root expressed as a path relative to the starting directory ('.' means the start directory is the root).

feature6_read_manifest.json.description: Reads and parses a project manifest (a JSON descriptor file) from a directory and returns the parsed object. The output lists the manifest's top-level field names (sorted) followed by each field's JSON-encoded value, so the parsed structure can be verified field-by-field.
